tweets.title: At the UN, India Calls for Accountability for Attacks on Children Addressing the UN Security Council, India's Permanent Representative Harish Parvathaneni said education is a fundamental right that must be protected even during armed conflict. He urged stronger accountability, stating that those who attack schools and target children must be held responsible. India highlighted alarming UN figures showing 38,558 grave violations against 24,174 children in 2025, including a 44% rise in attacks on schools, and reaffirmed its commitment to protecting children through initiatives such as the Right to Education and the DIKSHA digital learning platform.
tweets.content:
At the UN, India Calls for Accountability for Attacks on Children

The Army claims the seizure was carried out without following the prescribed Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs). In an effort to resolve the matter peacefully, the Commanding Officer reportedly sent a Company Commander and a Quick Reaction Team (QRT) to the police station to seek the release of the vehicles through dialogue. The Army alleges that police personnel locked the station gates and declined repeated requests for discussion. It further claims that after attempts at communication failed, the gates were breached. During the confrontation, a police official allegedly cocked his weapon and threatened the Army personnel, prompting the QRT to intervene, disarm the individual, and secure the area. A physical altercation followed, involving police personnel, including the Station House Officer (SHO). The Army maintains that the subsequent FIR filed against its personnel resulted from a breakdown in coordination between the civil administration and the Army. The matter remains under investigation, and the claims made by both sides have not been judicially established.

A major security scare has emerged from Chirawa, Rajasthan, after a man allegedly posing as an Indian Army Lieutenant Colonel and ECHS Vigilance Officer gained access to multiple private hospitals and examined sensitive records related to ex-servicemen and their families. The suspect reportedly convinced hospital administrations with his knowledge of military healthcare procedures and inspected OPD, admission, and treatment records before raising suspicion.

The alarm was triggered when hospital authorities noticed inconsistencies in his conduct and questioned his credentials. After ECHS officials intervened and attempted verification, the suspect failed to answer technical military questions and fled the area before he could be detained. A report has now been forwarded to Army Headquarters, while security agencies investigate whether this was an attempted fraud, data collection operation, or part of a larger network exploiting military identity.

The incident has exposed serious concerns regarding the misuse of military uniforms, unauthorized access to sensitive records, and verification protocols at institutions dealing with defence personnel and veterans.

tweets.title: DRDO’s Archer-NG UAV to Gain Strike Capability India's indigenous Archer-NG UAV is being developed beyond its surveillance role into a multi-role combat platform capable of engaging tanks, armoured vehicles, and even aerial targets. Key reported upgrades include: 1. Integration of anti-tank guided missiles (ATGMs) and Smart Anti-Airfield Weapons (SAAW) 2. Planned integration of the Astra Mk-1 beyond-visual-range air-to-air missile (BVRAAM) for engaging hostile aircraft 3. AESA radar to enhance target detection, tracking, and engagement 4. Retention of its primary intelligence, surveillance, target acquisition, and reconnaissance (ISTAR) role while adding precision-strike capability. tweets.content:
DRDO’s Archer-NG UAV to Gain Strike Capability

India's indigenous Archer-NG UAV is being developed beyond its surveillance role into a multi-role combat platform capable of engaging tanks, armoured vehicles, and even aerial targets.

Key reported upgrades include:

1. Integration of anti-tank guided missiles (ATGMs) and Smart Anti-Airfield Weapons (SAAW)

2. Planned integration of the Astra Mk-1 beyond-visual-range air-to-air missile (BVRAAM) for engaging hostile aircraft

3. AESA radar to enhance target detection, tracking, and engagement

4. Retention of its primary intelligence, surveillance, target acquisition, and reconnaissance (ISTAR) role while adding precision-strike capability.

If these capabilities are successfully integrated and validated, the Archer-NG will become one of India's first indigenous UAVs capable of performing both surveillance and offensive combat missions, significantly expanding the operational flexibility of the Indian Armed Forces.

DGCA and Air India have launched investigations. The airline says passenger safety was never compromised.

This shift comes after commercial talks with US-based General Electric (GE) hit a major roadblock over surging cost demands (nearly 300% higher than early estimates) and restrictive technology transfer terms.
tweets.content:
India is exploring joint engine development with France's Safran and the UK's Rolls-Royce for the AMCA fighter project. This shift comes after commercial talks with US-based General Electric (GE) hit a major roadblock over surging cost demands (nearly 300% higher than early estimates) and restrictive technology transfer terms.

Netram Defence Review (@NetramDefence)

GE demands 300% cost increase for AMCA engines.

India’s negotiations with GE Aerospace over the F414 fighter jet engine have reportedly stalled after the projected cost surged from ₹70–80 crore per engine to over ₹200 crore during commercial discussions.

The F414 is slated to power the HAL Tejas Mk2 and serve as the interim engine for the Advanced Medium Combat Aircraft (AMCA Mk-1).

- Engine cost up nearly 300%
- Proposed Indian assembly line could require over $800 million (~₹6,000 crore) in investment
- Negotiations continue over technology transfer, localisation, production, and support packages
- India is also evaluating alternative partnerships with Safran and Rolls-Royce
